title: Alfred Hitchcock Wanted Frank Lloyd Wright to Design the North by Northwest House: An Architect Just Built It for $45 Million
description:
in Architecture, Film, History |   	July 6th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Vil┬Łlains who live in opu┬Łlent, remote mod┬Łernist hous┬Łes may have been a cl
body:
in Architecture, Film, History |   	July 6th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Vil┬Łlains who live in opu┬Łlent, remote mod┬Łernist hous┬Łes may have been a clich├® since the last cen┬Łtu┬Łry, but giv┬Łen Hol┬Łly┬ŁwoodŌĆÖs addic┬Łtion to the tried and true, they do still turn up now and again. Unsur┬Łpris┬Łing┬Łly, few film┬Łmak┬Łers have man┬Łaged to use them any┬Łwhere near as┬Āmem┬Ło┬Łrably as Alfred Hitch┬Łcock did. Think back to┬ĀNorth by North┬Łwest, that show┬Łcase of both late-fifties high style and unadul┬Łter┬Łat┬Łed Hitch┬Łcock┬Łery, and any num┬Łber of images come right to mind: the dead┬Łly crop duster bear┬Łing down on Cary Grant, the hang off the edge of Mount Rush┬Łmore, the cheeky cut to the train enter┬Łing the tun┬Łnel. But on the archi┬Łtec┬Łtural┬Łly inclined, the deep┬Łest impres┬Łsion is made by not a shot but a set: the house ŌĆö mod┬Łernist, opu┬Łlent, remote ŌĆö occu┬Łpied by James MasonŌĆÖs vil┬Łlain Phillip Van┬Łdamm.
ŌĆ£The pio┬Łneer┬Łing deci┬Łsion to fea┬Łture a mod┬Łern house as the villainŌĆÖs lair in North by North┬Łwest arose from both the prac┬Łti┬Łcal needs of the script and the desire to explore inno┬Łva┬Łtion in archi┬Łtec┬Łtur┬Łal rep┬Łre┬Łsen┬Łta┬Łtion,ŌĆØ writes Chris┬Łtine Madrid French, author of The Archi┬Łtec┬Łture of Sus┬Łpense: The Built World in the Films of Alfred Hitch┬Łcock.
The look of the Van┬Łdamm House betrays con┬Łsid┬Łer┬Łable inspi┬Łra┬Łtion from the work of Frank Lloyd Wright, espe┬Łcial┬Łly his ŌĆ£icon┬Łic Falling┬Łwa┬Łter, best known for its aston┬Łish┬Łing pro┬Łject┬Łed porch┬Łes can┬Łtilevered over a run┬Łning stream.ŌĆØ As the Hol┬Łly┬Łwood sto┬Łry goes, Hitch┬Łcock asked Wright him┬Łself about the pos┬Łsi┬Łbil┬Łi┬Łty of design┬Łing the house, but when the archi┬Łtect asked for ten per┬Łcent of the filmŌĆÖs entire bud┬Łget, the job went to pro┬Łduc┬Łtion design┬Łer Robert F. Boyle.


	

Despite the high┬Łly un-Wright┬Łian steel beams sup┬Łport┬Łing the can┬Łtilevered liv┬Łing room (insert┬Łed because Grant need┬Łed a way to climb in), movie┬Łgo┬Łers left the the┬Łater assum┬Łing that theyŌĆÖd wit┬Łnessed a show┬Łdown in one of his hous┬Łes. In fact, like so many of Hitch┬Łcock┬ŁŌĆÖs famous built envi┬Łron┬Łments, the struc┬Łture did┬ŁnŌĆÖt actu┬Łal┬Łly exist: Boyle and his col┬Łlab┬Ło┬Łra┬Łtors con┬Łstruct┬Łed pieces on sets, com┬Łplet┬Łing the rest with mat┬Łte paint┬Łings. Yet their work did, in a sense, bring the Van┬Łdamm House into the world. A North by North┬Łwest fan since child┬Łhood, archi┬Łtect John Boc┬Łcar┬Łdo just this year achieved his $45 mil┬Łlion dream of build┬Łing it for real. Apart from faith┬Łful┬Łly repli┬Łcat┬Łing onscreen details, he also put in an eigh┬Łteen-seat home the┬Łater, pos┬Łsi┬Łbly on the safe assump┬Łtion that the buy┬Łer will be a fel┬Łlow cinephile ŌĆö who, giv┬Łen that the house over┬Łlooks Park City, Utah rather than sits atop Mount Rush┬Łmore, will sure┬Łly rue the day Sun┬Łdance decid┬Łed to move to Boul┬Łder. See pho┬Łtos here.
If you would like to sup┬Łport the mis┬Łsion of Open Cul┬Łture, con┬Łsid┬Łer mak┬Łing┬Āa dona┬Łtion to our site. ItŌĆÖs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬Łtri┬Łbu┬Łtions will help us con┬Łtin┬Łue pro┬Łvid┬Łing the best free cul┬Łtur┬Łal and edu┬Łca┬Łtion┬Łal mate┬Łri┬Łals to learn┬Łers every┬Łwhere. You can con┬Łtribute through Pay┬ŁPal, Patre┬Łon, and Ven┬Łmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
16 Free Hitch┬Łcock Movies Online
Alfred Hitch┬Łcock Explains the Plot Device He Called the ŌĆśMacGuf┬ŁfinŌĆÖ
Take a Tour of Frank Lloyd WrightŌĆÖs Ennis House, the Man┬Łsion That Has Appeared in Blade Run┬Łner, Twin Peaks & Count┬Łless Hol┬Łly┬Łwood Films
1,300 Pho┬Łtos of Famous Mod┬Łern Amer┬Łi┬Łcan Homes Now Online, Cour┬Łtesy of USC
A Med┬Łi┬Łta┬Łtive Tour of Falling┬Łwa┬Łter, Frank Lloyd WrightŌĆÖs Archi┬Łtec┬Łtur┬Łal Mas┬Łter┬Łpiece
Sal┬Łvador Dal├Ł Cre┬Łates a Dream Sequence for Spell┬Łbound, HitchcockŌĆÖs Psy┬Łcho┬Łan┬Ła┬Łlyt┬Łic Thriller
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: When Albert Einstein & Charlie Chaplin Met and Became Fast Famous Friends (1930)
description:
Pho┬Łto via Wiki┬Łme┬Łdia Com┬Łmons
ŌĆ£You do not real┬Łly under┬Łstand some┬Łthing unless you can explain it to your grand┬Łmoth┬Łer,ŌĆØ goes a well-known qu
body:
Pho┬Łto via Wiki┬Łme┬Łdia Com┬Łmons
ŌĆ£You do not real┬Łly under┬Łstand some┬Łthing unless you can explain it to your grand┬Łmoth┬Łer,ŌĆØ goes a well-known quote attrib┬Łuted var┬Łi┬Łous┬Łly to Albert Ein┬Łstein, Richard Feyn┬Łman, and Ernest Ruther┬Łford. No mat┬Łter who said it, ŌĆ£the sen┬Łti┬ŁmentŌĆ” rings true,ŌĆØ writes Michelle Lav┬Łery, ŌĆ£for researchers in all dis┬Łci┬Łplines from par┬Łti┬Łcle physics to ecopsy┬Łchol┬Ło┬Łgy.ŌĆØ As Feyn┬Łman dis┬Łcov┬Łered dur┬Łing his many years of teach┬Łing, it could be ŌĆ£the mot┬Łto of all pro┬Łfes┬Łsion┬Łal com┬Łmu┬Łni┬Łca┬Łtors,ŌĆØ The GuardianŌĆÖs Rus┬Łsell Gross┬Łman writes, ŌĆ£and espe┬Łcial┬Łly those who earn a liv┬Łing com┬Łmu┬Łni┬Łcat┬Łing the tricky busi┬Łness of sci┬Łence.ŌĆØ
Ein┬Łstein became one of the worldŌĆÖs great sci┬Łence com┬Łmu┬Łni┬Łca┬Łtors by choice, not neces┬Łsi┬Łty, and found ways to explain his com┬Łplex the┬Ło┬Łries to chil┬Łdren and the elder┬Łly alike. But per┬Łhaps, if heŌĆÖd had his way, he would rather have avoid┬Łed words alto┬Łgeth┬Łer, and pre┬Łferred acro┬Łbat┬Łic feats of silent dar┬Łing to get his mes┬Łsage across. We might at least con┬Łclude so from his rev┬Łer┬Łence for the work of Char┬Łlie Chap┬Łlin. Chap┬Łlin was the only per┬Łson Ein┬Łstein want┬Łed to meet in Cal┬Łi┬Łfor┬Łnia dur┬Łing his sec┬Łond, 1930ŌĆō31 vis┬Łit to the U.S., when he was ŌĆ£at the height of his fame,ŌĆØ notes Claire Cock-Starkey at Men┬Łtal Floss, ŌĆ£with news┬Łpa┬Łpers track┬Łing his every move and aca┬Łd┬Łe┬Łmics clam┬Łor┬Łing for expla┬Łna┬Łtions of his the┬Ło┬Łries.ŌĆØ
The admi┬Łra┬Łtion, of course, was mutu┬Łal. Their first meet┬Łings hap┬Łpened out┬Łside the pressŌĆÖs scruti┬Łny, at Uni┬Łver┬Łsal Stu┬Łdios, ŌĆ£where the pair took a tour and had lunch togeth┬Łer. They hit it off straight away, shar┬Łing quick wits and curi┬Łous minds.ŌĆØ In his auto┬Łbi┬Łog┬Łra┬Łphy, Chap┬Łlin writes that EinsteinŌĆÖs wife Elsa fina┬Łgled an invi┬Łta┬Łtion to din┬Łner at ChaplinŌĆÖs house. And he ŌĆ£was only too hap┬Łpy to oblige,ŌĆØ Cock-Starkey writes, arrang┬Łing an ŌĆ£inti┬Łmate din┬Łner, at which Elsa regaled him with the sto┬Łry of when Ein┬Łstein came up with his world-chang┬Łing the┬Ło┬Łry, some┬Łtime around 1915.ŌĆØ
The two con┬Łtin┬Łued to cor┬Łre┬Łspond, and the big pub┬Łlic unveil┬Łing of their friend┬Łship came when Chap┬Łlin invit┬Łed Ein┬Łstein to the pre┬Łmiere of City Lights in 1931 (see pho┬Łto up top) where the mega-celebri┬Łties from very dif┬Łfer┬Łent worlds were greet┬Łed by reporters, pho┬Łtog┬Łra┬Łphers, and ador┬Łing crowds. There are sev┬Łer┬Łal record┬Łed ver┬Łsions of their con┬Łver┬Łsa┬Łtion. In one account, Ein┬Łstein expressed bemuse┬Łment at the cheer┬Łing, and Chap┬Łlin remarked, ŌĆ£the peo┬Łple applaud me because every┬Łone under┬Łstands me, and they applaud you because no one under┬Łstands you.ŌĆØ
Chap┬Łlin him┬Łself wrote in his 1933ŌĆō34 trav┬Łel┬Łogue, A Come┬Łdi┬Łan Sees the World, that one of EinsteinŌĆÖs sons uttered the line, weeks after┬Łward: ŌĆ£You are pop┬Łu┬Łlar [because] you are under┬Łstood by the mass┬Łes. On the oth┬Łer hand, the professorŌĆÖs pop┬Łu┬Łlar┬Łi┬Łty with the mass┬Łes is because he is not under┬Łstood.ŌĆØ Yet anoth┬Łer ver┬Łsion, cir┬Łcu┬Łlat┬Łing on the Nobel PrizeŌĆÖs Insta┬Łgram and col┬Łlect┬Łing tens of thou┬Łsands of likes, has the exchange take place in a dia┬Łlogue.
Ein┬Łstein: ŌĆ£What I most admire about your art, is your uni┬Łver┬Łsal┬Łi┬Łty. You donŌĆÖt say a word, yet the world under┬Łstands you!ŌĆØ
Chap┬Łlin: ŌĆ£True. But your glo┬Łry is even greater! The whole world admires you, even though they donŌĆÖt under┬Łstand a word of what you say.ŌĆØ
What┬Łev┬Łer they real┬Łly said to each oth┬Łer, itŌĆÖs clear Ein┬Łstein saw some┬Łthing in Char┬Łlie Chap┬Łlin worth emu┬Łlat┬Łing. Chap┬Łlin left his mark on Exis┬Łten┬Łtial┬Łist phi┬Łlos┬Ło┬Łphy, lend┬Łing the name of his film Mod┬Łern Times to Jean-Paul Sartre and Simone de BeauvoirŌĆÖs influ┬Łen┬Łtial jour┬Łnal,┬ĀLes Temps Mod┬Łernes. He left a lega┬Łcy on Beat poet┬Łry, lend┬Łing the name┬ĀCity Lights to Lawrence FerlinghettiŌĆÖs infa┬Łmous San Fran┬Łcis┬Łco book┬Łstore and pub┬Łlish┬Łer. And it seems he also maybe had some small effect on physics, or on the most famous of physi┬Łcists, who might have har┬Łbored a secret ambi┬Łtion to be a silent film comedianŌĆöor to com┬Łmu┬Łni┬Łcate, at least, with the uni┬Łver┬Łsal effec┬Łtive┬Łness of one as skilled as Char┬Łlie Chap┬Łlin, favorite of genius┬Łes and grand┬Łmoth┬Łers (and genius grand┬Łmoth┬Łers) every┬Łwhere.
Note: An ear┬Łli┬Łer ver┬Łsion of this post appeared on our site in 2020.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:┬Ā┬Ā
60+ Free Char┬Łlie Chap┬Łlin Films Online
EinsteinŌĆÖs The┬Ło┬Łry of Rel┬Ła┬Łtiv┬Łi┬Łty Explained in One of the Ear┬Łli┬Łest Sci┬Łence Films Ever Made (1923)
Hear Albert Ein┬Łstein Read ŌĆ£The Com┬Łmon Lan┬Łguage of Sci┬ŁenceŌĆØ (1941)
The Char┬Łlie Chap┬Łlin Archive Opens, Putting Online 30,000 Pho┬Łtos & Doc┬Łu┬Łments from the Life of the Icon┬Łic Film Star
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬Łcian based in Durham, NC.

title: Cranbrook Academy of Art Reopens Applications for Fall 2026
description:
At Cranbrook Academy of Art, no two doors are exactly alike.
That was by design. Architect Eliel Saarinen believed even the smallest details deser
body:
At Cranbrook Academy of Art, no two doors are exactly alike.
That was by design. Architect Eliel Saarinen believed even the smallest details deserved thoughtful consideration. Each door across CranbrookŌĆÖs historic campus serves the same purpose, yet each possesses its own distinct characterŌĆöa reminder that creativity is found not in repetition, but in the possibilities created by difference.
With this spirit in mind, Cranbrook Academy of Art is reopening graduate applications for Fall 2026. Applications will be accepted from June 22 through August 15 for a limited number of available openings in select programs. For artists and designers considering graduate study, this additional application period provides another opportunity to join one of the nationŌĆÖs most distinctive graduate programs.
Students are encouraged to apply as early as possible to claim the limited remaining spots and merit-based scholarships.
Cherry Wood (MFA Photography 2027)
For nearly a century, Cranbrook has offered a graduate experience built around intensive studio practice, critical dialogue, and close mentorship. Every department is led by a single Artist-in-ResidenceŌĆöa practicing artist, designer, or architectŌĆöwhose commitment is to their own work and to the development of their students. Rather than following a standardized curriculum, students engage directly with accomplished practitioners in an environment that encourages experimentation, inquiry, and the pursuit of new ideas.
At Cranbrook, the studio is the classroom. The environment is the teacher.
Students are immersed in a community that challenges assumptions, expands perspectives, and encourages new ways of thinking. It is a place where conversations become collaborations, disciplines intersect, and ideas are tested through action, and action leads to innovation.┬Ā
Founded in 1932 and located on CranbrookŌĆÖs historic 320-acre campus in Bloomfield Hills, Michigan, just north of Detroit, the academy remains a place where creative practice and daily life are deeply intertwined. Designed by Saarinen, the campus continues to embody the belief that the built environment can shape how we learn, work, and create.
Irene Perez (MFA Fiber 2026) 
Cranbrook Academy of Art Library
Aaron Thompson (MFA Graphic Design 2026) 
Students come to Cranbrook seeking more than a degree. They come for the opportunity to immerse themselves in their practice, challenge assumptions, and develop work that reflects their unique voice and ambitions.
Applications open June 22 and close August 15, 2026. Financial aid is available, and all admitted students are considered for merit-based scholarships.
To learn more, visit cranbrookart.edu.

schema.headline: Cranbrook Academy of Art Reopens Applications for Fall 2026

title: Women Sport Puffer Coats in Bold Baroque Portraits by Nieves Gonz├Īlez
description: ŌĆ£Spain has an extraordinary, unbroken creative tradition: art, literature, music, research,ŌĆØ says Nieves Gonz├Īlez. From 16th-century portraitist E
body:
ŌĆ£Spain has an extraordinary, unbroken creative tradition: art, literature, music, research,ŌĆØ says Nieves Gonz├Īlez. From 16th-century portraitist El Greco to Baroque painters like Diego Vel├Īzquez and Bartolom├® Murillo, the nationŌĆÖs art history brims with narrative and intrigue. In the 17th century especially, dramatic contrasts of light and shadow influenced by Italian painter Caravaggio met movement, emotion, and religiosity to create theatrical tableaux.
For Gonz├Īlez, this legacy informs a painting practice that merges past and present. ŌĆ£Creating isnŌĆÖt something we do. ItŌĆÖs something we are,ŌĆØ the artist says in a statement. ŌĆ£And I come from that; I carry it in my body.ŌĆØ Her expressive oil compositions draw upon aristocratic portraits and religious paintings, centering the focus on beautiful women who wear contemporary parkas and puffer coats.
ŌĆ£Beati coloro che danzano allŌĆÖalba del giornoŌĆØ (2025), oil on linen, 116 x 97 centimeters
It would be remiss not to mention the AI-generated meme of Pope Francis wearing a giant white puffer coat, which swept across the internet in 2023 and appeared so realistic that it initially fooled many into thinking it was real. The stir was not only about the perils of the technology and deepfakes but also that the image itself was so jarring. The papacy originated approximately 2,000 years ago and is steeped in specific customs, rituals, and visuals, so to imagine the pope wearing something so modern felt out of sync with time and tradition.
This asynchronous tension is what Gonz├Īlez latches onto, albeit in a somewhat more tender, considerate way. She merges art historical tropes with an emphasis on women protagonists and garments that reflect style trends distinctive to the late 20th and 21st centuries. With demure expressions, vivid outfits, and direct gazes, the figures central in her paintings buck the norm.
ŌĆ£IŌĆÖve never tried to separate the cultured from the popular or the historical from the contemporary,ŌĆØ the artist says. ŌĆ£What interests me is precisely that these references speak to one another and generate something alive, not a nostalgic image of the past but something with a pulse todayŌĆ”But what ties all those layers together, if I had to name just one thing, is a search for humanity and emotion within the image. That whoever looks at the painting feels something real. ThatŌĆÖs what matters to me.ŌĆØ
A few of the works seen here are currently on view in the artistŌĆÖs solo exhibition, A Friendship Story, which continues through July 25 at Richard Heller Gallery in Santa Monica. See more on the artistŌĆÖs Instagram. You might also enjoy Ewa JuszkiewiczŌĆÖs reimagined historical portraits of women.
ŌĆ£The runaway IŌĆØ (2026), oil on canvas, 195 x 150 centimeters. Image courtesy of the artist and Richard Heller Gallery, Santa Monica
ŌĆ£For Each Other IIIŌĆØ (2026), oil on canvas, 40 x 50 centimeters. Image courtesy of the artist and Richard Heller Gallery, Santa Monica
ŌĆ£La PerlaŌĆØ (2026), oil on canvas, 70 x 50 centimeters
ŌĆ£La Santa y la FieraŌĆØ (2025), oil on canvas, 81 x 61 inches
ŌĆ£El gato negroŌĆØ (2025), oil on canvas, 116 x 81 centimeters
ŌĆ£EquinoxŌĆØ (2025), oil on canvas, 116 x 81 centimeters
ŌĆ£SomethingŌĆÖs crossed over in me and I canŌĆÖt go backŌĆØ (2026), oil on canvas, 150 x 130 centimeters. Image courtesy of the artist and Richard Heller Gallery, Santa Monica
ŌĆ£QuiteriaŌĆØ (2026), oil on canvas, 116 x 81 centimeters
ŌĆ£La santa y el VersoŌĆØ (2025), oil on canvas, 81 x 60 centimeters

title: Nebulae, Comets, and Aurorae, Oh My! See the ZWO Astronomy Photographer of the Year Shortlist
description: 769 photographers and astronomers around the world, representing 66 countries, submitted more than 4,000 images to this yearŌĆÖs ZWO Astronomy Photo
body:
769 photographers and astronomers around the world, representing 66 countries, submitted more than 4,000 images to this yearŌĆÖs ZWO Astronomy Photographer of the Year competition. The shortlisted photos represent a range of phenomena from aurorae and stars to planets and the moon, captured around the globe. Some photographers focus on the juxtaposition of space and the human environment while others take telescopically captured snapshots of distant galaxies and nebulae, creating striking composite images. 
Winners will be announced on September 17, the day after which the public exhibition will open at LondonŌĆÖs National Maritime Museum. The show is also accompanied by the book Astronomy Photographer of the Year, Collection┬Ā15.

title: Veks Van Hillik Suspends Fish, Insects, and Other Objects in Surreal Murals
description: In Dutch Golden Age still-life painting, itŌĆÖs not uncommon to be treated to tables laden with flowers and food such as fruits, game, and fish. The
body:
In Dutch Golden Age still-life painting, itŌĆÖs not uncommon to be treated to tables laden with flowers and food such as fruits, game, and fish. These works were painstakingly rendered; one can practically smell the sea. But the flip side is the temporality of these items, as the painting preserves their freshness, but we know they will ultimately decay. This incorporation of memento mori was intentional, as the inevitability of death was something people meditated carefully on.
Flora and fauna in Dutch painting also demonstrate abundance and diversity, from myriad types of foods to hyperrealistic flower arrangements, such as those of Rachel Ruysch, that may have had folkloric hidden meanings. For artist Veks Van Hillik, tropes from these canvases find their way into surreal, eccentric paintings and murals that also nod contemporary concerns and humanityŌĆÖs relationship to the natural world.

ŌĆ£There is also something almost systematic in the way humans tend to personify animals and project a certain form of anthropomorphism onto the stories we tell about them,ŌĆØ Van Hillik tells Colossal. ŌĆ£I enjoy playing with these unspoken codes inherited from fables and folk tales. And, whether intentionally or not, an ecological subtextŌĆöor at the very least a reflection on our ecosystemsŌĆöoften emerges in my work, something I like to preserve and contemplate.ŌĆØ
Fish emerged rather enigmatically as a central focus on Van HillikŌĆÖs work, partly because they were among the very first things he learned to draw as a child, when one of his older brothers taught him. ŌĆ£But the marine and aquatic world also offers an almost endless variety of forms, colors, and patterns,ŌĆØ he says. ŌĆ£Sometimes graceful, sometimes monstrous or grotesque, fish are almost always somewhat surreal.ŌĆØ
In his large-scale murals, and also increasingly in the studio, Van Hillik nods to the tones and settings of historical still life paintings, including sturdy surfaces, dark backgrounds, and architectural niches. Spilling from these are objects and animals we instantly recognize, such as birds, plants, and insects. But first impressions may be misleading, as upon closer inspection, a butterfly is missing its body, a nautilus balances on a bubble, and a heronŌĆÖs beak transforms into a key.
Van Hillik is interested in weight and presence along with unusual relationships and hybrid creatures. Fish, in particular, are the kind of thing we eat regularly, and some of us may even cast a line for them from time to time, but they live in a world entirely different from ours. The earthŌĆÖs aquatic expanses comprise a realm we are far from fully understanding; weŌĆÖve only mapped a little over a quarter of the entire sea floor, for example, and we really have no idea how many marine species there really are.

Along with what Van Hillik describes as their fascinating ŌĆ£peculiar sense of levitation,ŌĆØ fish are among some of his favorite animals to portray, representing both familiarity and mystery. Along with a range of other composite creatures, such as a tortoise shell-backed hare and a vivid, rather unsettlingly big-eyed goldfish with arms and legs, the artist leans into this sense of weightlessness and the uncanny, suspending plant stems, water droplets, and other objects in the air.
Van HillikŌĆÖs work was recently included in Common Waters at Arch Enemy Arts, where he is also slated for a solo show later this year. Follow updates on Instagram.

schema.headline: Veks Van Hillik Suspends Fish, Insects, and Other Objects in Surreal Murals

title: Vibrant Folded Canvases Form Garment-Like Sculptures by Susan Maddux
description: Drawing on her experience as a textile designer and her familyŌĆÖs artistic heritage, Susan Maddux investigates the relationships between fabric, pa
body:
Drawing on her experience as a textile designer and her familyŌĆÖs artistic heritage, Susan Maddux investigates the relationships between fabric, painting, and sculpture. If some of her longer pieces evoke the folds of kimonos, for example, itŌĆÖs not a coincidence. Tapping into both her Hawaiian upbringing and Japanese heritage, the artist creates vibrant works that drape on the wall like elegant cloaks hung up after a walk.
A small piece titled ŌĆ£MantleŌĆØ is redolent of the historical clothing worn to keep the shoulders warm. Other works feature conceptual or metaphysical names, such as ŌĆ£FlourishŌĆØ and ŌĆ£Thrum.ŌĆØ Maddux sometimes stacks the bunches of folds several feet high, mirroring human proportions so that, while abstract, there is a nuanced reference to the body and the timelessness of textiles.
ŌĆ£QuiverŌĆØ (2026), acrylic paint, canvas, and acrylic batting, 46 x 33 x 5 inches
With thick canvas folds, the artist emphasizes the power of repeated gestures and motifs, occasionally incorporating patterns and plumbing the tension between softness and firmness and what is concealed or revealed. SheŌĆÖs currently experimenting with the addition of different materials and new patterns following time spent at the RONDO art residency in Mexico City.
See more on the artistŌĆÖs Instagram.
ŌĆ£MimosaŌĆØ (2024), acrylic paint, canvas, archival adhesive, 21.5 x 19 x 2.5 inches
ŌĆ£FlourishŌĆØ (2026), acrylic paint, canvas, jute, batting, and archival adhesive, 67  x 38 x 4 inches
ŌĆ£MantleŌĆØ (2026), acrylic paint, canvas, and archival adhesive, 26 x 42 x 5 inches
ŌĆ£FacetŌĆØ (2025), acrylic paint, canvas, and archival adhesive, 30 x 18 x 3 inches
ŌĆ£HaloformŌĆØ (2026), acrylic paint, canvas, and archival adhesive, 66 x 24 x 5 inches
ŌĆ£MurmurŌĆØ (2026), acrylic paint, canvas, and archival adhesive, 48 x 31 x 6 inches
ŌĆ£VesperŌĆØ (2025), acrylic paint, canvas, and archival adhesive, 66 x 29 x 6 inches
ŌĆ£ThrumŌĆØ (2026), acrylic paint, canvas, and archival adhesive, 48 x 28 x 3.5 inches
Works in the studio
